refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation with end-to-end communication latency that ensures safe remote operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is mostly atomic, focusing on teleoperation latency and its compatibility with safety. The phrase 'compatible with safe remote operation' is kept to emphasize the safety aspect without specifying how latency is achieved or measured, maintaining solution neutrality. The FR does not contain multiple obligations requiring splitting.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure the end-to-end communication latency during teleoperation under agreed network conditions and verify that the latency meets the safety criteria defined in the worksite safety plan and operational policies, specifically that the p95 latency is ≤ 150 ms.

candidate_dps: Implement a communication protocol and system architecture optimized for low-latency end-to-end data transfer
Design a real-time data processing and transmission pipeline with prioritization and latency monitoring
Establish a latency budget allocation across system components with adaptive control to maintain latency targets
Use a modular communication interface that abstracts network conditions and supports latency-aware routing and error correction
selected_dp.selected_dp: Implement a communication protocol and system architecture optimized for low-latency end-to-end data transfer

potential_sources: Tight integration between communication protocol and control system timing
Dependency on network conditions and error handling mechanisms
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP focuses on the communication protocol and system architecture as a logical solution to achieve the latency requirement. It maintains independence from specific network technologies or hardware implementations, supporting modularity and adaptability.

assumptions_risks: Network conditions remain within agreed parameters to meet latency targets
Latency measurement and monitoring mechanisms are accurate and timely
Operational policies do not introduce additional delays or constraints impacting latency
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Consider explicitly including latency monitoring and adaptive control mechanisms in the DP to handle variability in network conditions and ensure consistent compliance with latency requirements.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Module responsible for remote control and communication aspects of the system, including protocol implementation for low-latency data transfer

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bi-directional data stream for real-time vehicle status and control feedback

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Commands and control instructions from higher-level systems

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical and logical network interface for data transmission

module_containment_scores.notes: Very broad module; contains many unrelated functions, leading to high diffusion of communication protocol logic.
Focuses on physical vehicle systems; communication protocol is peripheral, causing poor containment and high diffusion.
Directly responsible for communication and control; high cohesion and minimal diffusion.
Safety-related functions only; communication protocol partially relevant but not central, leading to moderate diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the most specific and cohesive boundary for implementing the communication protocol and system architecture optimized for low-latency data transfer, as it directly manages remote control and communication functions.
containment_risks: Potential dependency on Network Link interface stability and performance.
Integration challenges with Vehicle module for real-time data exchange.
Possible overlap with Safety Services if safety-critical communication is involved.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP directly addresses the FR by specifying a communication protocol and system architecture optimized for low-latency end-to-end data transfer, which is essential to support teleoperation with safe remote operation latency requirements.

diffusion_risks: Potential dependency on Network Link interface stability and performance.
Integration challenges with Vehicle module for real-time data exchange.
Possible overlap with Safety Services if safety-critical communication is involved.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The Teleoperation module shows high containment (score 9) and low diffusion (score 2), indicating strong cohesion and minimal scattering of the communication protocol logic. Other modules have higher diffusion scores, suggesting that Teleoperation is the best architectural boundary for this DP.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible through measuring end-to-end latency (p95 ≤ 150 ms) under agreed network conditions and ensuring compliance with worksite safety plans and operational policies.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The Teleoperation module is clearly defined as the implementation boundary responsible for the communication protocol and remote control, providing clear ownership and allocation of the FR through the DP to the IM.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define interface contracts and performance requirements for the Network Link to mitigate dependency risks.
Establish clear integration protocols with the Vehicle module to ensure real-time data exchange without increasing latency.
Clarify responsibilities and interfaces with Safety Services to avoid overlap and ensure safety-critical communication is properly managed within Teleoperation or Safety Services modules.

decision.justification: All gates (FR->DP, DP->IM, FR->IM) have passed successfully, indicating that the current functional requirement is adequately addressed by the design parameter and implementation module. The Teleoperation module shows strong cohesion and minimal diffusion, with clear ownership and feasible verification methods. Although there are some identified risks related to dependencies and integration, these are manageable through recommended revisions and do not necessitate further decomposition at this stage. Given the high safety criticality, maintaining a clear and contained implementation boundary is preferable to avoid complexity and potential errors.

revise.why: To explicitly define interface contracts and performance requirements for the Network Link, establish clear integration protocols with the Vehicle module, and clarify responsibilities and interfaces with Safety Services to mitigate identified dependency and overlap risks.

assumptions: The current DP sufficiently addresses the FR for low-latency teleoperation communication.
The Teleoperation module is the optimal implementation boundary with high containment and low diffusion.
Verification methods for latency and safety compliance are feasible and reliable.
open_risks: Dependency on Network Link interface stability and performance.
Integration challenges with Vehicle module for real-time data exchange.
Potential overlap with Safety Services for safety-critical communication.
evidence_needed: Detailed interface contracts and performance specifications for Network Link.
Integration protocols documentation with Vehicle module.
Clarified interface and responsibility definitions between Teleoperation and Safety Services modules.
